content: TIA diagnosis is clinical but requires urgent workup to confirm etiology and guide prevention. Neuroimaging: MRI brain with DWI within 24 hours—30-50% of clinically diagnosed TIAs show acute infarction (then classified as stroke, not TIA). Vascular imaging: carotid ultrasound or CTA/MRA head and neck to identify extracranial/intracranial stenosis, dissection, occlusion. Cardiac evaluation: ECG for atrial fibrillation, telemetry/Holter monitor (prolonged monitoring increases AF detection), echocardiogram (TTE/TEE) for cardioembolic sources (thrombus, PFO, valvular disease, cardiomyopathy). Laboratory: CBC, CMP, fasting lipid panel, HbA1c, coagulation studies. Hypercoagulable testing if young (<50) or suggestive history. EEG if seizure suspected (positive symptoms, postictal deficits). Differential diagnosis: seizure/Todd's paralysis (positive spreading symptoms, post-event confusion), migraine aura (gradual spread, positive symptoms, headache), hypoglycemia, peripheral vertigo, functional disorder. Risk stratification: ABCD2 score guides urgency (high-risk scores 6-7 warrant hospital admission); however, imaging findings (DWI positivity, significant stenosis) better predict recurrence than ABCD2 alone. Rapid access TIA clinics reduce time to treatment and stroke risk.
